text: The surgeon has fully mobilized the right colon and taken the mesentery to the planned transection sites. Using the Firefly imaging system, the surgeon ensures adequate perfusion to the planned anastomosis site. An anti-mesenteric suture is placed on both the colon and the small bowel to aid in the creation of the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a twelve millimeter right lower quadrant port for staple placement, cutting the suture to twenty centimeters to facilitate placement of two stay sutures, one at the current location and another eight centimeters distally.

The surgeon uses the robotic arm to hold the small bowel in place while preparing for an intracorporeal anastomosis. The suture is placed for orientation beneath the mesentery, revealing a wide defect for stapler passage. The colon is opened slightly medial toward the mesenteric side to facilitate stapler insertion, and a portion of the ligament of Treitz is removed. The small bowel is opened on the anti-mesenteric side, with suction applied as needed.

The surgeon positions the stapler with the thin side towards the small bowel and the large side towards the colon, ensuring alignment with minimal twisting. Holding the stay suture, the mesentery falls away, allowing the full length of the stapler to be inserted for a wide anastomosis. The stapler is lifted anteriorly to the abdominal wall to ensure the mesentery is clear before closing and firing the stapler.

The surgeon reaches in to grasp the back wall of the common enterotomy colotomy, ensuring the second stapler fully removes the specimen side tissue. This action is performed distal to the proximal stay suture, with visible perfusion up to the planned transection site and lack of perfusion on the specimen side. The distal stay suture aids in orienting the specimen for a perpendicular staple across the original staple line. The mesentery is fully mobilized to prevent bleeding during the second stapler firing.
The surgeon places the resected specimen into a large thoracic endo catch bag through a fifteen millimeter port at the extraction site.
